old_claim.note: Human Anatomy & Physiology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Paré's ligatures, amputations, wound treatment, and prosthetic work depended on practical anatomical knowledge of vessels, limbs, and traumatic injuries.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica identifies Pare as a major Renaissance surgeon and describes his treatment of gunshot wounds, replacement of boiling-oil treatment, and reintroduction of tying large arteries rather than cauterizing vessels during amputation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews Pare's practical surgical innovations around wounds, arteries, amputations, and Renaissance surgery.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Those procedures require anatomical knowledge of vessels and limbs, so anatomy is retained as an enabling prerequisite without treating it as the whole innovation.

ontology_before: Surgical Advances (Pare) depended on Human Anatomy & Physiology with only a generic inferred enabling claim.
ontology_after: Surgical Advances (Pare) keeps Human Anatomy & Physiology as the source-backed anatomical substrate for ligature, amputation, wound treatment, and prosthetic work.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: human_anatomy_physiology remains a direct enabling prerequisite with textbook evidence.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed only to pharmacy or wound-dressing recipes with no surgical manipulation of vessels, limbs, or injuries.
A stronger source showed Pare's ligature and amputation practice did not rely on practical anatomy.
A more specific Renaissance surgical anatomy node were introduced and should replace the broader human anatomy prerequisite.
short_reason: Pare's arterial ligature and amputation practice make anatomy a sourced enabling context.
